summary: Gruber evaluates AT&T's new tiered data plans for smartphones and iPad, weighing the positives against the negatives. He praises the lower prices for most users (98% use under 2GB) and the reasonable overage fees on the main plan, but criticizes the usurious DataPlus overage charges, the $20 tethering fee that provides no extra bandwidth, and the abrupt change to iPad data terms just 30 days after launch. He also considers the broader implications: lower monthly bills could expand iPhone market share, and even with AT&T's flawed tethering pricing, it's still cheaper than carrying a separate MiFi device. His overall verdict is that the good outweighs the bad.
key_points: The DataPro plan saves most users $5/month since 98% use under 2GB, with reasonable $10/GB overage fees compared to Verizon and Sprint's $50/GB
AT&T's tethering policy is indefensible: charging $20/month for no additional bandwidth on an already capped plan is pure rent-seeking
Changing iPad data terms just 30 days after launch undermines trust in a flagship product's highly touted unlimited plan
Lower monthly minimums ($55 vs $70) remove a bigger barrier to iPhone adoption than handset pricing, potentially expanding market share
iPhone OS 4 multitasking will increase bandwidth usage through background streaming apps like Pandora and Skype, making these caps more relevant
money_quotes: If you use more than 2 GB per month, you deserve to pay more than the rest of us who do not. Why is this hard to understand?
If you don't get extra bandwidth, what are you paying for?
It's one thing to charge extra for tethering on an 'unlimited' data plan, but it's outrageous to charge $20 when the bandwidth is already capped.

What kind of company has a data plan for a flagship product — what appears to be the flagship product of the entire industry this year — available for just 30 days before changing the terms significantly?
Apple wants the iPhone to be perceived as an affordable luxury item, not a cheap or free gadget.
The biggest barrier for many consumers, I think, is the size of the monthly bill.
AT&T's $20 charge just to enable tethering is bullshit.
So while AT&T's new plans are not entirely good news, the good outweighs the bad overall.
key_insight: AT&T's tiered data pricing is fundamentally sound for most users, but the tethering surcharge for zero additional bandwidth and the bait-and-switch on iPad terms reveal a carrier still prioritizing rent extraction over customer trust.
